title: Document systemd resource control for Quadlet containers
body:
### Feature request description

I would like to set memory and CPU limits for Quadlet container, but cannot find documentation what's the "correct" / recommended way to do this.

Following advice from the Quadlet / Podman announcement blog post:

> Notice the `[Container]` section. You just need to specify the `Image` you want to run and the command (`Exec`) that you want to run, and then you can use all of the other fields normally specified in a systemd unit file.[^1]

[^1]: https://www.redhat.com/en/blog/quadlet-podman#podman-4.4-merges-quadlet-into-podman

I tried:
```ini
[Container]
CPUQuota=200%
MemoryMin=4G
MemoryMax=4G
```

But I got an error in `journalctl --user -b`:

> [...] systemd[8884]: quadlet-generator[8884]: [...] unsupported key 'CPUQuota' in group 'Container' [...]

Searching `podman-systemd.unit(5)` I could not figure out whether or how I should apply the knobs from `systemd.resource-control(5)` to `~/.config/containers/systemd/*.container` files.

Searching the GitHub issues, I found:

> applying the memory limit to the systemd service works only if you also specify `--cgroups=split` to reuse the current cgroup, otherwise that limit applies to the Podman command not the container payload
> If you want a limit for the container then you should use `PodmanArgs=--memory 4G`. It is not a workaround but the correct solution.[^2]

[^2]: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/22521#issuecomment-2082075299

But also an apparently contradictory statement in a different issue:

> With quadlet, you can manage things like cgroups in the Systemd [service] section rather then manage it via podman.
> I believe we should allow systemd to manage the cgroup, it also controls the podman service and any other tools executed.
> 
> For example in Quadlet we do.[^3]
> ```ini
> [Service]
> ... no `--cgroups=split` ...
>
> [Container]
> ... no `--cgroups=split` ...
> ```

[^3]: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/20499#issuecomment-1783217053

> I think we should rely on Systemd for setting this up or just use podmanargs to pass it down. Having both will just confuse things.[^4]


[^4]: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/20499#issuecomment-1847212308

### Suggest potential solution

https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/20499#issuecomment-1847214772 suggests:

> Perhaps we should enhance the Quadlet man page to make it easier to discover settings for it.

I am asking for this documentation.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

I am using `PodmanArgs=--cpus=2 --memory=4g --memory-reservation=4g` now, but I am not sure whether this is the recommended way to apply limits, since it appears to circumvent systemd, while @rhatdan's statement[^3] seems to imply I should not do that, and `podman-systemd.unit(5)` says:

> PodmanArgs=
> Since the generator is unaware of what unexpected interactions can be caused by these arguments, it is  not  recommended to use this option.[^5]

[^5]: https://docs.podman.io/en/stable/markdown/podman-systemd.unit.5.html#podmanargs

### Additional context

_No response_

comments.body:
> But also an apparently contradictory statement in a different issue

Why - I think - it seems contradictory is that the example doesn't include `ExecStart=`, which is where `--cgroups=split` is passed as an argument to `podman run` as generated by quadlet. Look into `/run/systemd/generator` for examples of how the resulting units look.

As for where you should apply the the resource control options, that's described by this sentence from systemd.resource-control(5):

`The resource control configuration options are configured in the [Slice], [Scope], [Service], [Socket], [Mount], or [Swap] sections, depending on the unit type.`

So for quadlet generated service units you pass them in the `[Service]` section.

> > But also an apparently contradictory statement in a different issue
> 
> Why - I think - it seems contradictory is that the example doesn't include `ExecStart=`, which is where `--cgroups=split` is passed as an argument to `podman run` as generated by quadlet. Look into `/run/systemd/generator` for examples of how the resulting units look.

Thanks!  I found the unit at `/run/user/$(id -u)/systemd/generator/*.service`.  It appears `--cgroups=split` is now applied by default and does not have to be set by me as https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/22521#issuecomment-2082075299 suggested, correct?

> As for where you should apply the the resource control options, that's described by this sentence from systemd.resource-control(5): [...]
> So for quadlet generated service units you pass them in the `[Service]` section.

Do you mean the Quadlet systemd unit generator / Podman will pick it up from there and apply it as a limit to the container?

Then the following is no longer correct?

> If you want a limit for the container then you should use `PodmanArgs=--memory 4G`.[^1]

[^1]: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/22521#issuecomment-2082075299

I just tried removing `Container.PodmanArgs` and adding `Service.MemoryMax` instead, and it does not appear to work: `podman stats` shows the container with `MEM USAGE / LIMIT` being the host's amount of memory, not the `MemoryMax=4G` that I set in the `.container` unit.

> Thanks! I found the unit at /run/user/$(id -u)/systemd/generator/*.service. It appears --cgroups=split is now applied by default and does not have to be set by me as https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/22521#issuecomment-2082075299 suggested, correct?

I don't know if this changed between then and now with quadlet, or if that was just a general statement, but yes you don't need to set this yourself at the moment as far I'm aware.

> Do you mean the Quadlet systemd unit generator / Podman will pick it up from there and apply it as a limit to the container?

> podman stats shows the container with MEM USAGE / LIMIT being the host's amount of memory, not the MemoryMax=4G that I set in the .container unit.

No, the generator will just pass the option into the resulting .service file unaltered, therefore it is configured on the unit, but this information is not (at least not currently) passed to the `podman run`, so as far as `podman run` is concerned, no memory limit was set (so you don't see it in `podman stats`), but it is in effect. As mentioned, this has the added effect that the setting limits both the container processes and all other processes started as part of the service, for example the `podman run` process itself.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
It would, indeed, be great if podman stats could read the systemd limits and show it accordingly.
